fact: May 25 is exactly 145 days into the year - that's almost exactly 40% of the whole year done!
In the Northern Hemisphere, this is prime time for baby birds to be learning how to fly, so you might see lots of clumsy crash landings in your backyard.
The sun rises before 6 AM in most northern places by this date, making it one of the earliest sunrise times of the year.
May 25 falls smack in the middle of Gemini season - the zodiac sign of the twins, which explains why the weather can't decide if it wants to be hot or cool.
In Australia and other Southern Hemisphere places, this date marks the heart of autumn when leaves are turning brilliant orange and red.
Dandelions are going absolutely crazy around this time, shooting their fluffy seed parachutes everywhere like nature's confetti cannons.
The moon can appear extra orange or red when it's low in the sky during late May evenings because of all the pollen and dust particles in the air.
We're only about 26 days away from the summer solstice, so daylight is stretching longer every single day by about 2 minutes in northern regions.
Fireflies start blinking their butts around this time in many places - it's like nature's own disco party beginning.
In cold mountain areas, this is when the last stubborn patches of snow finally give up and melt, creating rushing waterfalls and streams.
May 25 is prime allergy season in many places because tree pollen is mixing with early grass pollen - basically a sneeze festival.
The Earth is tilted at just the right angle by this date that northern places get almost 15 hours of daylight while southern places get only about 9 hours.
